posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Anthony hosted us at his home last night for Monday Table, to explore La Romanée a bit further. His wife Kay, allowed us to explore the vast array of her cooking talents, providing a splendid meal to accompany a fine set of wines.

We kicked off with a bottle of 2001 Château Coutet Cuvée Madame. Anthony was channeling his inner Richard Olney and paired it with freshly shucked oysters. It was rich, unctuous and layered. There were notes of apricot, tinned peaches, honey and vanilla. It had incredible intensity and length and finished with good freshness. The iodine character of the oysters worked really well with the wine.

After a cheeky little Krug and a brace of G-Bangers it was onto the main event. A wedgie bracket of two '02’s had a DRC ringer.

1992 Krug Champagne Clos du Mesnil: There’s a hint of nutty development sneaking into the aroma profile. It also smells of custard apple and vanilla. In the mouth there’s green apple tang and a big burst of flavour upfront, with a tight minerally tail. The finish is dry and chalky.

2013 Weingut Keller Riesling G-Max: The nose is a ripe rockmelon with diesel poured over it. The palate is rich, dense and chewy. There are notes of exotic fruits and smoke and it has good build and a finish that oozes minerality.

2014 Weingut Keller Riesling G-Max: This is open for business and quite charming. There were whiffs of nettle, smoke, sweet melons and slate. The palate is full and fruity with a steely spine. It has good balance and proportion and finishes cool and dry.

2015 Weingut Keller Riesling G-Max: So tight and compact, but so much energy and depth. There’s a whiff of fruit salad and a wisp if smoke. It has serious build and is layered with minerals and fruit. It has a high glycerol feel but is light on its feet. Length is superb.

1993 Bouchard Père et Fils La Romanée, Grand Cru: Mild TCA ruined what could have been a beautiful bottle. There were still floral notes and crisp berry and cherry fruits evident. It had good depth and mineral line and fine persistence.

2003 Bouchard Père et Fils La Romanée, Grand Cru: One of the very best ‘03’s I’ve had. A luscious, succulent wine where the fruit is shining. It has a core of black cherry and some cooler menthol notes. There’s a suggestion of sarsaparilla and it is quite velvety against the gums. It is complex, deep and long.

2004 Bouchard Père et Fils La Romanée, Grand Cru: A bit green and mean. There are some pleasant notes of sandalwood and floral spice, but it lacks flesh and finishes a tad hard.

2002 Bouchard Père et Fils La Romanée, Grand Cru: Some V.A. punches floral spice aromas into the nostrils. Fruits vacillate between red and black and it is a luscious and full wine of nice flavour and texture.

2002 Domaine de la Romanée-Conti Romanée-Conti, Grand Cru: Complex and ethereal with a beautiful perfume of rose petals, freshly grated ginger, pomegranate, cherry and sandalwood. It is cool and elegant in the mouth, with great finesse and a lacy texture. Flavours titillate, and nothing dominates. It is calm and engaging, with excellent presence and an airy feel. It has terrific persistence.

2005 Bouchard Père et Fils La Romanée, Grand Cru: Deep, dark, spicy and luscious. It has great fruit intensity and shows some camphor from the wood. It has great drive and is carried by sweet, spherical tannins.

2006 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: A very pretty nose of rose petals, cherry and freshly grated ginger. It is mid-weight in the mouth, with good balance and excellent persistence.

2007 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: Plenty of floral punch to the aroma. It is full, sweet and juicy in the mouth. There are layers of fruit, flora and mineral and the finish is expansive and long. A very good ’07.

2008 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: Badly corked.

2009 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: Plump, opulent and giving. Red and black fruits are trimmed with floral spice. There’s a sandalwood note too and something smoky. It is intense, deep and extremely long.

2010 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: Complex and ethereal but relatively tight at this stage. There are notes of red currant, cherry and pomegranate. It has a sweet entry to the mouth and builds, expanding right through the palate. It finishes with cool minerality and floral nuance and is so silky against the gums. It is a wine of ultimate class and refinement.

1925 Stanley Special Reserve Tawny Port: A single barrel that was bottled in 1968. This had a lovely, fine spirit and complex fruit smells and flavours. It had notes of sweet raisin, honey, grilled nuts and chocolate. It was very sweet, intense and rich, but not heavy in the slightest and possessed phenomenal length.

posts.name: Alex Rychlewski
posts.cooked:
Worth the money?

I’m not being (especially) jealous here, it’s just that at those prices your notes don’t seem to reflect, any sort of electroshock, knocked your proverbial socks off…

Alex R?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Alan, there are 12 members in the group and we all have a turn each year at hosting. The host chooses the theme and generally the participants will all bring a bottle of the theme wine. If the participant doesn’t have a theme wine the host may accept a Champagne, white or other wine of a similar value to use on the night, or may sell the participant a bottle.

Charlie, the '13 is the most developed, the '14 is probably the most pleasant now and is nicely balanced. The '15 is the best wine, but is very tight and piercing now. I’m glad I have now tried G-Max. I won’t be chasing them at the sort of money they now command.

Alex, many of us in the group have bought the wines we consume on release or at wholesale pricing. Some of the wines we had last night were absolutely worth the money that was paid for them.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Slumming it without Montrachet and only one Romanee Conti I see. What a night - even within the Monday Night oeuvre! Great notes, and thanks for the vicarious thrills.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Interesting to see what more age does to this wine…I suspect that the '15 will (with a lot of time time) be something quite amazing from the bottle we saw last night.

Still, am with Jeremy here, having now tried the G-Max, I wouldn’t be rushing out to buy some.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Secondary Keller prices have gone nuts.

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the write up.

I was once offered a bottle of 2012 La Romanee at an indecent price. Which I turned down.

Would love to try a good bottle of I-Max once to see what the fuss is all about.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Sanjay Nandurkar:

Thanks for the write up.

I was once offered a bottle of 2012 La Romanee at an indecent price. Which I turned down.

Would love to try a good bottle of I-Max once to see what the fuss is all about.

Sanjay,

The 2012 La Romanee is an absolutely incredible wine, seems like from what I tasted last night and recently that LB’s wines have been getting better and better every year (maybe starting to really hit the heights from 2009).

The '12’s, '14’s and '16’s we tasted at the cellar were all sensational, right across the range.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
Russel,

yes – Keller prices have gone nuts. And there were some people on internet boards which had nothing else in mind as to establish a new luxury brand. I had some battles with a guy on the old Parker Board about exactly this issue. The guys have been successful. I am German and I drink a lot of Riesling. G-Max is not better than Hubacker for instance. Or Wagner Stempel and many, many others. When I read that people are willing to pay 1.000 dollars plus for a bottle of G-Max or even more I doubt that things are going the right direction in this world. Sorry that I must say this – but its ridiculous to pay that amount of money for a bottle of G-Max. There were many other fine Rieslings from Germany with the same quality. This Keller hype has at least absolutely nothing to do with the fact that Keller is in a league of his own. He is not. He produces fine wines as many others in Germany. No more, no less.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Jurgen,

I certainly agree. Wines such as Dönnhoff’s Niederhäuser Hermannshöhle Riesling GG is every bit as good as these G-Max wines for mine.

Cheers
Jeremy

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: I wish I could agree.
